perturbation.original_text: 6.3 The Receiving Party shall comply with any requests in terms of 6.1 and/or 6.2 above within five "Business Days " (being any day other than a Saturday, Sunday or official public holiday in the Republic of South Africa) of the date of such a request.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.3 The Receiving Party shall comply with any requests in terms of 6.1 and/or 6.2 above within twenty "Business Days " (being any day other than a Saturday, Sunday or official public holiday in the Republic of South Africa) of the date of such a request.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction by modifying the timeframe within which the receiving party must comply with a request for the return or destruction of confidential information. Originally, compliance was required within five business days; the modification extends this period to twenty business days. This creates uncertainty about the enforceability of the original deadline, especially in cases where a swift return of information is critical to mitigating potential damages, creating a direct contradiction with 4.2.5.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4.2.5 immediately notify the Disclosing Party in writing if it becomes aware that there has been an unauthorised disclosure or use of any Confidential Information and take all necessary steps to recov er such Confidential Information and to prevent any further disclosure of such Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the compliance timeframe from five to twenty business days. This contradicts section 4.2.5, which requires the receiving party to 'immediately notify' the disclosing party of unauthorized disclosures and take 'all necessary steps to recover' confidential information. The extended timeframe for complying with requests under 6.1 and/or 6.2 directly conflicts with the immediacy required by 4.2.5, creating an in-text contradiction as defined by the 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' perturbation type. While Section 6.3 deals with requests to return or destroy the Confidential Information, extending the time for compliance can be seen as conflicting with section 4.2.5 as the unauthorised disclosure might necessitate immediate action instead of waiting for the extended deadline for compliance.

perturbation.original_text: 8.1 this Agreement does not create or record any legal oblig ation of any kind whatsoever to disclose any information or conclude or negotiate towards the 6 implementation of the Transaction and such obligations shall only come into existence if the Parties agree thereto in writing in a separate document; and
perturbation.changed_text: 8.1 this Agreement does create a legal oblig ation of any kind whatsoever to disclose any information or conclude or negotiate towards the 6 implementation of the Transaction and such obligations shall only come into existence if the Parties agree thereto in writing in a separate document; and
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a direct contradiction by negating the statement that the agreement doesn't create any legal obligation to disclose information. The original clause states that no such obligation exists unless agreed upon separately. The modified clause asserts that such an obligation does exist. This directly contradicts clause 2.4, which states the disclosing party is under no obligation to disclose any confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.4 The Receiving Party acknowledges that the Disclosing Party is under no obligation to disclo se any Confidential Information or any other information or documentation relating to the Transaction to the Receiving Party, notwithstanding the conclusion of this Agreement. The Receiving Party further acknowledges that, a s a condition to receiving a ny documentation and/or information relating to the Transaction , the Receiving Party is required to conclude this Agreement and bind itself to the undertakings herein.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in section 8.1 states that the agreement creates a legal obligation to disclose information. This directly contradicts section 2.4, which explicitly states that the disclosing party is under no obligation to disclose any confidential information. The change creates a clear and direct conflict, leading to an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 9.1 Subject to 9.3, in the event of the Receiving Party committing any breach of the terms of this Agreement, the Disclosing Party shall be entitled , by giving a written notice to this effect to the Receiving Party, to forthwith cancel this Agreement or claim specific performance of the Receiving Party's obligations, without prejudice to its rights to claim damages or such other remedy as the law may allow.
perturbation.changed_text: 9.1 Subject to 9.3, in the event of the Receiving Party committing any breach of the terms of this Agreement, the Disclosing Party shall be entitled, by giving a written notice to this effect to the Receiving Party, to only claim specific performance of the Receiving Party's obligations, without prejudice to its rights to claim damages or such other remedy as the law may allow.
perturbation.explanation: This perturbation introduces an in-text contradiction by removing the Disclosing Party's right to cancel the agreement in the event of a breach by the Receiving Party. The original text allows the Disclosing Party to either cancel the agreement or claim specific performance. The modified text removes the option of cancellation, limiting the Disclosing Party's remedies. This contradicts Section 4.4 which allows for relief by way of interdict, which implicitly acknowledges breaches beyond simply specific performance, and contradicts the right to interdict mentioned in Section 4.4.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4.4 The Receiving Party agrees that monetary damages may not be a sufficient remedy for a breach of the undertakings given by it in this 4 and accordingly, without prejudice to any other rights and remedi es available to the Disclosing Party, agrees that the Disclosing Party shall, in the event of a breach of any of the provision s of this Agreement, be entitled to seek relief by way of interdict, specific performance or otherwise. 4
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 9.1 limits the Disclosing Party's remedies to only claiming specific performance, removing the option to cancel the agreement in the event of a breach by the Receiving Party. This directly contradicts Section 4.4, which states that the Disclosing Party is entitled to seek relief by way of interdict or specific performance in the event of a breach. By removing the option to cancel, Section 9.1 now limits the Disclosing Party's ability to seek relief as outlined in Section 4.4, creating a strong contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 13.3 This Agreement shall in all respects be governed by the laws of the Republic of South Africa .
perturbation.changed_text: 13.3 This Agreement shall in all respects be governed by the laws of England and Wales.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a critical in-text contradiction by altering the governing law of the agreement. The original text specifies the laws of the Republic of South Africa, while the changed text designates the laws of England and Wales. This has implications for the interpretation and enforcement of the entire agreement, including dispute resolution (13.4), potentially rendering the arbitration clause unenforceable depending on the laws of England and Wales.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 13.4 In the event of any dispute or difference arising between the Parties r elating to or arising out of this Agreement, including the implementation, execution, interpretation, rectification or cancellation of this Agreement, the dispute shall be referred for arbitration to the Arbitration Foundation of Southern Africa (or its su ccessor -in-title) ("AFSA ") in terms of AFSA 's Rules of Arbitration for the time being in force. The seat, or legal place, of the arbitration shall be Johannesburg , Republic of South Africa and the language to be used in the arbitral proceedings shall be E nglish.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies section 13.3 to specify that the agreement is governed by the laws of England and Wales, whereas the original text stated that it was governed by the laws of the Republic of South Africa. Section 13.4 specifies that disputes will be referred to the Arbitration Foundation of Southern Africa (AFSA), located in Johannesburg, Republic of South Africa. Changing the governing law directly contradicts the location and rules of the arbitration clause, creating a strong in-text contradiction as the arbitration clause is tied to South African law, which is no longer the governing law according to the modified clause. This change breaks compatibility and makes enforcement unclear.
